learning_outcomes.description: Apply the basic modelling of power system components for steady state system analysis
Apply the appropriate methodologies to solve power system steady state operating conditions
Solve load flow problems of large power systems with appropriate models of transmission line, transformer, generator and loads
Analyse symmetrical and unsymmetrical faults in power systems
Explain why stability is important to normal operation of power systems
Apply basic stability techniques into power system analysis
Solve power system analysis problems using PSS/E software to fulfil certain specifications of generation, transmission and demand
Explain basics of power systems structure in a deregulated environment
Explain Power Distribution network and voltage regulation

assessments.assessment_title: Hardware experiments and simulation by PSS/E
assessments.due_date:
PSS/E simulation 1: 20/03/2026 4:00 pm

PSS/E simulation 2: 17/04/2026 4:00 pm

PSS/E simulation 3: 8/05/2026 4:00 pm

PSS/E simulation 4: 29/05/2026 4:00 pm

Two Hardware Experiments: 29/05/2026 4:00 pm
assessments.task_description:
Hardware experiments: Two practical hardware experiments will be performed by the students at the Industry 4.0 Energy TestLab in Hawken Engineering Building S202. More information about the schedule is available in Blackboard. There will be no report submission for the laboratory experiments. Students' work in the laboratory will be assessed during the lab sessions. Each lab will have 3% of marks and total marks will be 6% for two experiments.


Use of AI for Hardware Experiments

This assessment task (Hardware experiments) is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.


Simulation: Students will complete four assignments using computer simulations on transmission line modelling for steady state power flow, load flow, short circuit analyses and transient stability using the PSS/E software. Students will be required to submit FOUR reports for the PSS/E simulation. Each simulation report will carry 6% marks and total marks for the simulation will be 24% for four reports.

  • PSS/E simulation -1: Transmission line modelling for steady state power flow 
  • PSS/E simulation -2: Load flow 
  • PSS/E simulation -3: Short circuit analyses (both balanced and unbalanced) 
  •  PSS/E simulation -4: Transient stability


Use of AI for Simulation

This assessment (Simulation by PSS/E) task evaluates student’s abilities, skills and knowledge in specific technical topics. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: PSS/E Simulation reports are to be submitted through the course Blackboard Online system.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
16/04/2026 2:10 pm

The exam will take place during the scheduled Applied Class.
assessments.task_description:
In-semester exam is based on the contents covered on the topic of Transmission and Distribution Line: Transmission and Distribution Line Modelling and Analysis.

The exam will be scheduled during the applied class time of 16 April 2026. This is a 60-minute invigilated in-semester exam. More information will be provided through Blackboard.

Use of AI

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: In class submission at the end of exam time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
The final exam will examine the full course, with a special emphasis to transmission line, load flow, fault analysis, transient stability, distribution systems, and electricity market structure.

The exam will be a closed book exam. Students will be required to use Casio fx-82 series or UQ approved and labelled non-programmable calculator for this exam. Students are not allowed to use computer and any software to solve the problems. No marks will be given if the students use computer to solve problems.

Assessment types will include short answer, short essays and problem solving.

Use of AI

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Lectures on various topics

Series of lectures covering transmission and distribution system modelling, load flow analysis, balanced and unbalanced fault analysis and transient stability analysis for bulk power systems. Challenges in power system due to energy transition and electricity market structure in Australia will also be introduced. Two lectures will be delivered by the industry experts.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L08, L09
learning_activities.topic_description: Lectures on various topics Series of lectures covering transmission and distribution system modelling, load flow analysis, balanced and unbalanced fault analysis and transient stability analysis for bulk power systems. Challenges in power system due to energy transition and electricity market structure in Australia will also be introduced. Two lectures will be delivered by the industry experts.

learning_activities.topic:
Applied Class

Applied class will start from the second week of semester. Applied class sessions will take place weekly during the whole semester (5/3-2/4, 16/4-28/5) except the mid-semester break and public holidays. Students will improve their problem solving skill during the applied class sessions, where ELEC4310 teaching staff will assist students with discipline expert knowledge. Problem solving skill will be assessed during the in-semester and final examinations.



Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Hardware laboratory experiment and Simulation by PSS/E software

Use PSS/E software to solve power system problems and hardware experiments in the Industry 4.0 Energy Test laboratory. Details of hardware experiment sessions this will be announced through course Blackboard site.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06, L07
